tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1.–1.6.) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie znajdz w tekscie wyrazy lub wyrazenia, ktore odpowiadaja definicjom podanym w punktach 1.7.–1.11. Wymagana jest calkowita poprawnosc ortograficzna odpowiedzi.
tasks.text:
THE PUBLIC LIBRARY: A PLACE FOR EVERYONE

The public library as we know it today has a surprisingly short history. Although libraries have existed for thousands of years, for most of that time they were the exclusive domain of the wealthy, the clergy, or those connected to universities. It was not until the nineteenth century that the idea of a library freely open to all citizens began to gain widespread support.

In Britain, the Public Libraries Act of 1850 marked a pivotal moment. For the first time, local authorities were permitted to use public funds to establish and maintain libraries that anyone could use without charge. The Act was initially controversial. Many critics argued that providing free access to books would encourage idleness among the working classes, while others questioned whether taxpayers should foot the bill for other people's leisure. Despite this opposition, the movement grew steadily, and by the end of the century hundreds of towns across Britain had opened their own public libraries.

The American philanthropist Andrew Carnegie played a decisive role in expanding access to libraries on both sides of the Atlantic. Between 1883 and 1929, Carnegie donated money to build over 2,500 libraries across the English-speaking world. His motivation was personal: as a young immigrant, he had been able to educate himself largely through free access to a private library in Pittsburgh. He believed that libraries gave everyone, regardless of their background, the opportunity to better themselves. Carnegie's contributions came with conditions, however. The local community was required to provide the land and to commit to funding the library's ongoing running costs from public taxes.

Modern public libraries face considerable challenges. The rise of the internet has led some to question whether physical libraries are still necessary in an age when information is available at the touch of a screen. Lending figures for physical books have fallen in many countries, and local governments facing budget cuts have been tempted to reduce library funding or close branches altogether. However, libraries have adapted by expanding their range of services. Many now offer digital lending, computer access, children's reading programmes, language classes, and meeting spaces for community groups. Far from becoming obsolete, public libraries have reinvented themselves as community hubs where people can access not just books, but services and support that may not be available to them elsewhere.

items.statement: Libraries freely open to all citizens existed in Britain before the nineteenth century.
items.explanation: FALSE — The text states that 'It was not until the nineteenth century that the idea of a library freely open to all citizens began to gain widespread support'.

items.statement: The Public Libraries Act of 1850 was immediately welcomed by all sections of British society.
items.explanation: FALSE — 'The Act was initially controversial': critics argued against free access and questioned the use of public money.

items.statement: Carnegie's personal experience as a young person influenced his decision to fund libraries.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'His motivation was personal: as a young immigrant, he had been able to educate himself largely through free access to a private library in Pittsburgh'.

items.statement: Communities that received Carnegie's funding were not expected to contribute anything financially.
items.explanation: FALSE — Communities 'were required to provide the land and to commit to funding the library's ongoing running costs from public taxes'.

items.statement: The number of physical books borrowed from libraries has risen in many countries in recent years.
items.explanation: FALSE — 'Lending figures for physical books have fallen in many countries'.

items.statement: Modern public libraries now provide a wider range of services than simply lending books.
items.explanation: TRUE — Libraries 'now offer digital lending, computer access, children's reading programmes, language classes, and meeting spaces for community groups'.

vocabItems.definition: to pay the cost of something on behalf of others
a person who gives money to support a cause or institution
of great importance, with a significant effect on how things develop in the future
no longer useful or needed because something newer has replaced it
a central point where people come together for activities or services
